claim: In Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight steels, nanosized κ-carbide, (Fe,Mn)3AlCx, has the E2₁ cubic perovskite crystal structure and maintains a cube-on-cube orientation relationship with the austenite matrix: ⟨100⟩κ // ⟨100⟩γ and {001}κ // {001}γ.
evidence_text: nanosized j-carbide, $(\mathrm{F e},\mathrm{M n})_{3}\mathrm{Al C}_{x},$ which has the $\operatorname{E 2}_{1}$ cubic perovskite crystal structure ... j-Carbide has a cube-on-cube orientation relationship with the austenite matrix: $\langle 1 0 0\rangle_{\kappa} / / $ $\langle 1 0 0\rangle_{\gamma}$ and $\smash{\{0 0 1\}_{\kappa} / /\{0 0 1\}_{\gamma}}$

claim: Precipitation of κ-carbide in the temperature range 450–700°C results from compositional modulation produced by spinodal decomposition into carbon-rich and aluminum-rich/depleted zones, which may be preceded or accompanied by short-range ordering.
evidence_text: precipitation of j-carbide in the temperature range of 450–700-C is the result of compositional modulation produced by spinodal decomposition into either carbon rich or carbon and aluminum rich and depleted zones. Spinodal decomposition is thought to either precede, take place concurrently or subsequently to short-range ordering (SRO) into lattice sites corresponding to the j-carbide structure.

claim: In Fe-30Mn-9Al-1Si-0.9C steels aged at 530°C, increasing phosphorus from 0.001% to 0.043% greatly accelerates aging kinetics and raises peak hardness; after 100 h, hardness values are ~325 HBW (0.001% P), ~345 HBW (0.006% P), ~380 HBW (0.018% P), and ~370 HBW (0.043% P).
evidence_text: Phosphorus is shown to greatly increase both the hardening rate and the peak hardness during aging of a 1% Si steel as shown in Fig. 2b.

claim: The predicted increase in yield strength due to spinodal hardening in Fe-30Mn-9Al-XSi-0.9C-0.5Mo cast steels aged at 530°C for 60 h is approximately 120 MPa per wt.% Si.
evidence_text: The predicted increase in the yield strength, as determined using a spinodal hardening mechanism, was calculated to be 120 MPa/wt.% Si for specimens aged at 530-C for 60 h

claim: Phosphorus substitution for aluminum in κ-carbide reduces the cleavage stress by 45%, which is the predicted mechanism for notch toughness degradation in aged Fe-Mn-Al-C steels containing phosphorus.
evidence_text: Phosphorus substitution for aluminum in the j-carbide structure ... relaxes the local bonding character along h100ij. This results in a 45% reduction in the cleavage stress of the j-carbide and is predicted to be the mechanism behind the reduction in notch toughness of aged Fe-Mn-Al-C steels containing phosphorus.

claim: A calcium-treated Fe-30Mn-9Al-1.07Si-0.9C steel with 0.001% P, solution treated 2 h at 1050°C, exhibits hardness 197 HB, 0.2% YS 452 MPa, UTS 737 MPa, and 64% elongation (Table II).
Phosphorus segregation during solidification increases quench sensitivity and promotes precipitation of κ-carbide along austenite grain boundaries.
evidence_text: The quench sensitivity of these lightweight steels will also be increased by segregation of phosphorus during solidification and the phosphorus will promote precipitation of j-carbide along the austenite grain boundaries.

claim: Calcium and misch metal (Ce, La) additions followed by argon stirring increase the room-temperature CVN impact energy of the aged alloy from 33 J to 108 J, mitigating phosphorus embrittlement by forming Ce/La-P complex inclusions and reducing AlN density.
evidence_text: The room-temperature CVN impact energies of an aged Fe-30Mn-9Al-1Si-0.9C-0.3Mo alloy increased from 33 J, in the untreated condition, to 108 J with calcium and misch metal additions followed by argon stirring.

claim: Aging-induced brittle cleavage fracture in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels proceeds by glide plane decohesion on {111} slip planes, facilitated by planar slip and strain localization in shear bands resulting from κ-carbide precipitation.
evidence_text: Acselrad et al.40 reported that aging produced a brittle transgranular cleavage-like fracture in which cleavage facets were found to contain linear markings along two or three different directions. They proposed that the mechanism of brittle cleavage fracture with aging was the result of ''slipping off,'' or glide plane decohesion, along the slip plane producing a {111}-type fracture facilitated by planar slip and shear strain localization in shear bands resulting from j-carbide precipitation.

claim: In the as-hot-rolled condition, room-temperature CVN impact energy was 23 J (L-T) and 22 J (T-L) with transgranular cleavage fracture showing slip traces at ~60° angles; after solution treatment at 1100°C for 10 min and water quenching, toughness increased to 160 J (L-T) with fully ductile fracture.
evidence_text: In the as-hot-rolled condition, the breaking energy was only 23 J in the L–T orientation and 22 J in the T–L orientation. ... Solution treating for 10 min at 1100°C and water quenching greatly improved toughness to an average of 160 J (L–T orientation), and fracture was completely ductile... The cleavage facets displayed slip traces at roughly 60- angles from one another indicating fracture by decohesion of active slip planes.

claim: The dynamic fracture toughness J_Id was determined from instrumented Charpy tests using the Schindler single-specimen technique, requiring the peak load (Pmax), energy up to peak load (Emax), and total fracture energy (Etot).
evidence_text: Schindler43 has devised a single-specimen technique for determining the dynamic fracture toughness J_Id from instrumented Charpy tests. The only experimental inputs necessary for this technique are the peak load (Pmax), the energy consumed up to the peak load (Emax), and the total fracture energy (Etot).

claim: In the solution-treated condition, the dynamic fracture toughness (DFT) was statistically equivalent between low phosphorus (<0.003% P) 1.07 and 1.56% Si specimens, with an average value around 700 kJ/m^2.
evidence_text: In the solution-treated condition, the dynamic fracture toughness was statistically equivalent between the low phosphorus (<0.003% P) 1.07 and 1.56% Si specimens with an average value around 700 kJ/m2.

claim: Brittle fracture is attributed to an increase in size, carbon content, and possibly volume fraction of κ-carbide during aging with increasing Si, which concentrates planar slip on fewer slip bands and promotes {111} glide-plane decohesion, especially at high strain rates.
evidence_text: Brittle fracture is thus attributed to an increase in the size, carbon content, and perhaps volume fraction of j-carbide during aging with increasing amounts of silicon... As silicon has been shown to increase the size and carbon content of j-carbide during aging, this would also contribute to an increase in dislocation accumulation on relatively few slip bands and increases the propensity of {111} decohesion, especially at high strain rates.

claim: The 0.59% Si steel contained more than twice the porosity of the 1.07% Si steel and almost double that of the 1.56% Si steel, while total inclusion densities and sizes were similar among the three low-P silicon steels (Fig. 15).
evidence_text: the only major difference in inclusion content between the different silicon containing steels was the amount of porosity. The porosity in the 0.59% Si steel was more than twice that of the 1.07% Si steel and almost double that of the 1.56% Si steel. ... The total inclusion density was similar between the three low-phosphorus silicon-containing steels with between 62 and 78 inclusions/mm2.

claim: The lowered dynamic fracture toughness of the aged 0.59% Si low-P steel is likely due to its higher amount of porosity, as porosity in high-strength steel was reported to decrease toughness by more than 50 kJ/m^2.
evidence_text: It is therefore likely that the lowered toughness of the aged 0.59% Si sample is the result of an increased amount of porosity. ... porosity in high-strength steel has been shown to deleteriously affect notch toughness and was reported to decrease the toughness of quenched and tempered Cr and Mo steel by more than 50 kJ/m2.

claim: In the solution-treated condition and with low phosphorus (<0.007% P), Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C-(0.59–1.56)Si steels have high work-hardening rates, strengths up to 737 MPa, and dynamic fracture toughness over 700 kJ/m^2.
evidence_text: In the solution-treated condition and for steels with low phosphorus (<0.007% P), these steels have high work-hardening rates with strengths up to 737 MPa and dynamic fracture toughness over 700 kJ/m2.

claim: As silicon increased from 1.07% to 1.56% in age-hardened steels, Charpy V-notch toughness decreased from 92 J to 68 J and dynamic fracture toughness decreased from 376 kJ/m^2 to 265 kJ/m^2.
evidence_text: As the addition of silicon was increased from 1.07% to 1.56% in age-hardened steels, the Charpy V-notch toughness decreased from 92 J to 68 J and the dynamic fracture toughness decreased from 376 kJ/m2 to 265 kJ/m2, respectively.

claim: Phosphorus sharply reduces toughness: in solution-treated condition, CVN impact energy drops from 200 J at 0.006% P to 28 J at 0.07% P; in aged steels with ~1% Si, increasing P from 0.001% to 0.043% decreases DFT from 376 kJ/m^2 to 100 kJ/m^2.
evidence_text: Toughness was found to be a strong function of phosphorus, decreasing the solution treated CVN impact toughness from 200 J in a 0.006% P steel to 28 J in a 0.07% P steel with a nominal silicon content of 1% Si. For aged steels with nominally 1% Si, increasing the levels of phosphorus from 0.001% to 0.043% decreased the dynamic fracture toughness from 376 kJ/m2 to 100 kJ/m2.

claim: Further improvement in toughness requires grain refining techniques to reduce cast austenite grain size or cast preforms that can be grain refined by forging, and research is needed to develop dispersoids that interfere with planar slip.
evidence_text: Further improvement to these steels will require grain refining techniques to reduce the cast austenite grain size or cast preforms that can subsequently be grain refined by forging. Furthermore, there is a need to develop dispersoids capable of interfering with the planar slip induced by either SRO or shearing of j-carbide to improve notch toughness and fatigue properties.
